title: Getting Started with Hexagonal Architecture
subtitle: A practical guide to building maintainable applications
excerpt: Learn how to structure your application using hexagonal architecture to keep business logic independent of frameworks and infrastructure.
content:
Hexagonal architecture, also known as ports and adapters, is a software design pattern that aims to create loosely coupled application components.

The core idea is to isolate the business logic (the hexagon) from external concerns like databases, UIs, or third-party services.

$image1

By defining ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations), you can easily swap out dependencies without changing the core domain.

This approach is particularly useful for long‑lived projects where technology stacks evolve over time.

$image2

In this article we'll walk through a concrete example using TypeScript and Next.js.

$image1.caption: The core hexagon with surrounding adapters

seoDescription: A step-by-step guide to implementing hexagonal architecture in TypeScript and Next.js for maintainable applications.

title: Dark Mode Aesthetics in Modern Web Design
subtitle: Creating calm, readable interfaces for night owls
excerpt: Explore the principles behind effective dark mode design and how to implement a palette that reduces eye strain.
content:
Dark mode isn't just a trend—it's a usability feature that can reduce eye strain and save battery life on OLED screens.

$image1

Choosing the right background color is crucial. Pure black (#000) can cause harsh contrasts, while dark grays (#1a1a1a) are often more comfortable.

Accent colors should be carefully selected to maintain readability and visual hierarchy.

$image2

We'll look at concrete CSS variables and design tokens you can adopt in your own projects.

$image2.caption: Pure black vs dark gray backgrounds

seoDescription: Learn how to create effective dark mode interfaces with proper color contrast and accessibility in mind.

title: Building a Static Blog with Next.js and JSON
subtitle: No database, no CMS—just files
excerpt: How to create a fast, SEO‑friendly blog using Next.js App Router and local JSON files as the content source.
content:
Static blogs are making a comeback, and for good reason: they're fast, secure, and easy to deploy.

$image1

With Next.js 14 and the App Router, we can build a fully static site that still supports dynamic features like pagination and search.

All content lives in local JSON files, making it easy to version and edit without a database.

$image2

This article walks through the architecture, from file structure to deployment on Vercel.

$image2.caption: From local JSON to live static site

seoTitle: Static Blog with Next.js and JSON | Slowpokita
seoDescription: Step‑by‑step tutorial for building a performant static blog using Next.js App Router and local JSON files.
